---
title: "Chain statistics, likelihood, and fitting"
subtitle: "Reproducing the epichains R package functionality"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
engine: julia
---

## Overview

Transmission chain simulation, chain statistics, likelihood computation,
and offspring distribution fitting with **EpiBranch.jl** are demonstrated
here, reproducing and extending the core functionality of the
[epichains](https://github.com/epiverse-trace/epichains) R package.

```{julia}
using EpiBranch
using Distributions
using DataFrames
using Random: Xoshiro
```

## Simulating transmission chains

The basic workflow: define an offspring distribution and a generation time,
then simulate. Each initial case seeds an independent transmission chain.

```{julia}
# Simulate 20 independent chains with Poisson(0.9) offspring
model = BranchingProcess(Poisson(0.9), Exponential(5.0))

rng = Xoshiro(42)
state = simulate(model;
    sim_opts = SimOpts(n_initial = 20, max_cases = 10_000),
    rng = rng
)

println("Total cases: ", state.cumulative_cases)
println("Extinct: ", state.extinct)
```

## Chain size and length

Size (total cases) and length (number of generations) for each chain are
computed by `chain_statistics`:

```{julia}
cs = chain_statistics(state)
first(cs, 10)
```

Summary statistics:

```{julia}
println("Mean chain size: ", round(mean(cs.size), digits = 2))
println("Max chain size: ", maximum(cs.size))
println("Mean chain length: ", round(mean(cs.length), digits = 2))
```

## Analytical chain size distributions

For Poisson offspring, the chain size follows a Borel distribution.
For Negative Binomial offspring, it follows a distribution derived via
Lagrange inversion:

```{julia}
# Borel distribution for Poisson(0.9) offspring
d = chain_size_distribution(Poisson(0.9))
println("Chain size distribution: ", typeof(d))
println("Mean chain size (analytical): ", round(mean(d), digits = 2))

# For NegBin offspring
d_nb = chain_size_distribution(NegBin(0.8, 0.5))
println("NegBin chain size distribution: ", typeof(d_nb))
```

## Likelihood of observed chain sizes

Given observed chain sizes, the log-likelihood under different offspring
distribution parameters can be computed. Wrap the data in `ChainSizes`:

```{julia}
# Observed chain sizes (e.g. from contact tracing data)
observed = ChainSizes([1, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1, 5, 1, 2])

# Log-likelihood under different parameters
for λ in [0.5, 0.9, 1.5]
    ll = loglikelihood(observed, Poisson(λ))
    println("LL at λ=$λ: ", round(ll, digits = 2))
end
```

With Negative Binomial offspring:

```{julia}
ll_nb = loglikelihood(observed, NegBin(0.9, 0.5))
println("LL for NegBin(R=0.9, k=0.5): ", round(ll_nb, digits = 2))
```

## Likelihood with imperfect observation

In practice, not all cases are observed. Wrap the model in
`PartiallyObserved` to marginalise over the true (unobserved) chain
sizes:

```{julia}
base = BranchingProcess(Poisson(0.9))
ll_full = loglikelihood(observed, PartiallyObserved(base, 1.0))
ll_partial = loglikelihood(observed, PartiallyObserved(base, 0.7))

println("LL (full observation): ", round(ll_full, digits = 2))
println("LL (70% observation): ", round(ll_partial, digits = 2))
```

## Simulation-based likelihood

When interventions are present, simulation-based likelihood can be used.
Pass a `BranchingProcess` model instead of a distribution:

```{julia}
model = BranchingProcess(Poisson(0.9), Exponential(5.0))
ll_sim = loglikelihood(observed, model; n_sim = 5_000, rng = Xoshiro(42))
ll_analytical = loglikelihood(observed, Poisson(0.9))
println("LL (simulation-based): ", round(ll_sim, digits = 2))
println("LL (analytical):       ", round(ll_analytical, digits = 2))
```

Simulation-based likelihood also works with interventions:

```{julia}
iso = Isolation(delay = Exponential(2.0))
model_with_iso = BranchingProcess(Poisson(2.0), Exponential(5.0))

ll_with_iso = loglikelihood(ChainSizes([1, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1, 5, 1, 2]), model_with_iso;
    interventions = [iso],
    attributes = clinical_presentation(incubation_period = LogNormal(1.5, 0.5)),
    n_sim = 5_000,
    rng = Xoshiro(42)
)
println("LL with isolation: ", round(ll_with_iso, digits = 2))
```

Likelihood evaluation under interventions is not available in the
R **epichains** package.

## Fitting offspring distributions

Use `fit` to find the maximum likelihood offspring distribution.
The same interface works for all data types — it extends `Distributions.fit`:

```{julia}
# From offspring counts (direct secondary case observations)
offspring_data = OffspringCounts([0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 1, 0, 2, 5, 0])
d_pois = fit(Poisson, offspring_data)
d_nb = fit(NegativeBinomial, offspring_data)
println("Poisson MLE: R = ", round(mean(d_pois), digits = 2))
println("NegBin MLE:  R = ", round(mean(d_nb), digits = 2), ", k = ", round(d_nb.r, digits = 2))
```

```{julia}
# From chain sizes (subcritical only)
d = fit(Poisson, observed)
println("Poisson MLE from chain sizes: R = ", round(mean(d), digits = 2))
```

## Chain length likelihood

Chain length likelihoods are available analytically for Poisson and
Negative Binomial offspring:

```{julia}
lengths = ChainLengths([0, 1, 0, 2, 1, 0, 0, 3, 0, 1])
ll_len = loglikelihood(lengths, Poisson(0.5))
println("Chain length LL: ", round(ll_len, digits = 2))
```

## Finite population (susceptible depletion)

For outbreaks in small populations, susceptible depletion can be added
to the branching process:

```{julia}
model_finite = BranchingProcess(Poisson(2.0), Exponential(5.0);
    population_size = 500)

rng = Xoshiro(42)
state = simulate(model_finite;
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 1000),
    rng = rng
)

println("Cases: ", state.cumulative_cases, " / 500 population")
```

## Comparing with analytical extinction probability

Simulation results can be validated against analytical solutions:

```{julia}
R, k = 1.5, 0.5
q_analytical = extinction_probability(R, k)

rng = Xoshiro(42)
model = BranchingProcess(NegBin(R, k), Exponential(5.0))
results = simulate_batch(model, 5000;
    sim_opts = SimOpts(max_cases = 10_000, max_generations = 200),
    rng = rng
)
q_simulated = containment_probability(results)

println("Extinction probability (analytical): ", round(q_analytical, digits = 4))
println("Extinction probability (simulated):  ", round(q_simulated, digits = 4))
```
